ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for whole roasted duck with salt-and-pepper duck rack combo; FOODON_03302051 (duck food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers duck meat generically; no specific code for whole roasted duck or salt-and-pepper preparation exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for whole roasted duck with salt-and-pepper duck rack combo dish.

serving.description: 1 whole duck with duck rack (shared meal for 4–6 persons)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of this combo dish is approximately 1,200 g total (whole roasted duck plus the salt-and-pepper duck rack), intended as a shared meal for 4–6 people at a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (USDA SR Legacy #05141) combined with estimated salt-and-pepper fried duck rack component
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values are drawn from USDA SR Legacy entry for roasted duck (meat and skin). The salt-and-pepper duck rack component is a fried preparation of the duck carcass with seasoning; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect added salt and soy-based marinade typical of this preparation. The composite per-100g values represent a weighted blend of roasted whole duck flesh (approximately 70% by edible weight) and the fried/seasoned duck rack (approximately 30% by edible weight). Energy and macros are consistent with USDA FDC data for roasted duck with skin.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Protein- and Fat-Dominant Foods — Meat and Poultry Reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for whole roasted duck or salt-and-pepper duck rack. Duck is a high-protein, high-fat, very low-carbohydrate food (0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). By analogy with other roasted poultry and meat entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables (Atkinson et al., 2008), which consistently show GI values of approximately 0–15 for protein-dominant meat dishes, a GI of 10 is assigned. GL per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (10 × 6 g) / 100 = 0.6, where carbohydrate per 1,200 g serving ≈ 6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from added salt, soy-based marinade, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g; a single shared portion can contribute 500–900 mg sodium per person, approaching or exceeding 40% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content but high saturated fat and total fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The negligible carbohydrate content (0.5 g per 100 g) means this dish has minimal direct glycemic impact; however, the high saturated fat content (9.5 g per 100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, so portion control and infrequent consumption are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in total fat (28 g per 100 g), saturated fat (9.5 g per 100 g), cholesterol (89 mg per 100 g), and sodium (480 mg per 100 g), all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with existing CVD or dyslipidaemia should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Whole Duck with Salt and Pepper Duck Rack (全只鸭配椒盐鸭架) is a premium Chinese restaurant combo dish consisting of two preparations from the same duck: a whole roasted duck and a separately prepared salt-and-pepper duck rack (carcass). The whole duck is typically marinated with soy sauce, five-spice powder, garlic, and sesame oil before being roasted until the skin is crisp and mahogany-coloured, yielding tender, richly flavoured meat with a characteristic savoury-sweet glaze. The duck rack — the bony carcass remaining after the breast and leg meat are removed — is seasoned with salt, white pepper, and sometimes chilli, then deep-fried until golden and crunchy, making it a popular snack-style accompaniment. Together, the combo offers contrasting textures: the silky, fatty roasted duck flesh against the crispy, intensely seasoned fried rack. This dish is a signature offering at Cantonese and Chinese roast-meat restaurants in Singapore, often ordered for family gatherings or celebratory meals. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (approximately 337 kcal per 100 g), high in protein (19 g per 100 g), and very high in fat (28 g per 100 g), with significant saturated fat (9.5 g per 100 g) and sodium (480 mg per 100 g) from the seasoning and marinade. Carbohydrate content is negligible (0.5 g per 100 g), giving it a very low glycemic index. Clinically, the high sodium and saturated fat content make it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or dyslipidaemia. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets and contains soy and sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for roasted duck (meat and skin), which is an approved primary source. The salt-and-pepper duck rack component required a weighted composite estimate with sodium adjustment for seasoning. No exact match for this specific combo dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database methodology for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate meat dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite of roasted whole duck (70% edible weight share) and fried salt-and-pepper duck rack (30% edible weight share), adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 (duck, domesticated, meat and skin, roasted). Sodium is adjusted upward from the USDA baseline (89 mg/100 g) to approximately 480 mg/100 g to reflect the salt, soy sauce, and seasoning typical of restaurant preparation. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for roasted duck. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; duck meat and skin contain no dietary fibre. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; cooked duck meat contains no measurable vitamin C. 5. GI of 10 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. This dish is sold as a combo set at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜), Circular Road, Singapore, priced at SGD 98.80. 7. The dish is not halal-certified based on available information; halal status should be verified with the restaurant. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) present in poultry fat at trace levels per USDA data.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record from USDA SR Legacy source for roasted duck. Constructed composite nutrient profile for whole duck + salt-and-pepper rack. Added all multilingual names for Singaporean Chinese cuisine (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Assigned GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney methodology. Populated all health context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg) and zero values (dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg) in notes.

Iteration 2: Verified GI category threshold (GI=10 ≤ 55 → 'low' confirmed). Verified all ontology match_type values are within allowed set. Verified zero-macro justification phrases present in notes. Confirmed aliases consistency — all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases array. Confirmed natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 pass.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
